actions.thought: I am on the Button/SB with T2o, which is a very weak hand (bottom 15-20% of hands). Although I have position and heads-up ranges are wide, T2o has poor playability and low equity realization. Since this is the first hand and I have no reads on the opponent's tendencies, adopting a conservative approach by folding the bottom of my range is prudent to avoid marginal situations. The pot odds to limp are attractive, but T2o often leads to difficult post-flop spots. Folding is the standard GTO play for this specific hand.

J3o is a very weak hand even in a heads-up or small blind versus big blind scenario. While I am on the button, this hand lacks connectivity and high-card strength. Folding is the most disciplined play to avoid being put in difficult spots post-flop with a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 9c 4d. This is a heads-up match, 100bb deep. While I have position post-flop, 94o is one of the weakest hands in the deck (bottom 10-15%). At this stack depth, opening this hand is generally -EV compared to folding. It is too disconnected and low to realize equity effectively, and I don't want to start the match bleeding chips with the bottom of my range. I will fold.
83o is a very weak hand even in heads-up play, lacking both connectivity and high-card value. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In Heads-Up play, 57s is a very playable hand from the button (SB). The previous four hands have resulted in folds from the small blind, indicating a passive dynamic so far. I want to break this pattern and apply pressure. 57s has good playability post-flop due to flush and straight potential. A min-raise to 200 allows me to steal the blinds if the opponent is folding too much, or play a pot in position with a hand that connects well with boards.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Five offsuit. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 200, which is a standard 2x open. While the price to call is relatively cheap (100 more to win a total pot of 300), J5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I prefer to fold and wait for better opportunities rather than defending with such a marginal hand.

In a heads-up situation, J6o is a playable hand from the button. Having position throughout the hand allows me to play more aggressively. I will put in a standard opening raise to 300 to exert pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Q4o facing a 3x raise (to 300) from the Button. While Q4o has roughly 40-45% raw equity against a wide heads-up opening range, I am out of position and the hand has poor playability (bad kicker, poor connectivity). I need 33% equity to call (200 to win 600), but due to being out of position, I will likely under-realize my equity. Against a 3bb open, Q4o is a standard fold.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 6c 2h, which is effectively trash (62o). In a standard heads-up opening range, this hand falls well below the threshold for raising or even limping. It has poor equity, poor playability, and negative implied odds. Given the stack depths and the fact that it is early in the match, there is no strategic merit in trying to steal with the bottom of my range. I will simply fold.

Holding Queen-Eight suited (Qd 8d) on the Button in a heads-up game is a strong starting hand, well within my opening range. So far, the game has been relatively tight, with most pots ending pre-flop. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and take control of the hand, as I have the positional advantage for the rest of the betting rounds.

In a heads-up scenario, A4o is a reasonable hand to defend from the Big Blind against a Button open. The opponent has raised to 3x (300 chips). Getting pot odds of 2:1 (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600), I need roughly 33% equity. A4o has solid equity against a wide button opening range (often 50-80% of hands). While a 3-bet is an option due to the Ace blocker, calling is the standard, lower-variance play to realize equity without bloating the pot out of position against a 3x open.

I am out of position on a J-T-7 rainbow board with A4o (Ace-high). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, connecting well with broadways and pairs. I have no pair and no meaningful draw. Leading out (donk betting) would be ill-advised as I have neither value nor a strong semi-bluff. The standard line is to check to the raiser and react to their action. If they bet, I will likely fold given my lack of equity.

On a Jack-Ten-Seven board, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine) and a backdoor diamond flush draw with my Queen of diamonds. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow up with a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to maintain pressure and capitalize on any folds from the opponent, while still having significant equity to improve on later streets.

I hold Ace-Four offsuit on a J-T-7 rainbow board. I checked to the pre-flop raiser, who bet 300 (half pot). This board texture interacts very heavily with a standard button opening range (broadways, connectors), meaning the opponent likely has a pair or a strong draw. With only Ace-high, no flush draw, and extremely thin straight potential (backdoor only), I have very poor equity. I need 25% equity to call, which I likely do not realize out of position against a range that connects this well. Floating here is negative EV. The disciplined play is to fold.

In a heads-up match, King-high hands on the button have decent equity (~53%) against a random hand and shouldn't be folded. While limping is an option to control the pot size with a marginal hand, raising to 200 allows me to seize the initiative, potentially take down the pot pre-flop, and play the hand aggressively in position if called. Given the previous passive history where many pots ended pre-flop, a standard min-raise is appropriate to steal the blinds.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Four offsuit. The opponent in the Small Blind has raised to 200. While I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (100 more to win a total pot of 300), J4o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Given the early stage of the match and the lack of connectivity or high-card strength of my hand, I choose to fold and wait for a more favorable spot to commit chips.

I hold Ace-Ten offsuit on the button in a heads-up match, which is a premium starting hand. I have a slight chip lead and want to put pressure on the big blind. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to build the pot and potentially take it down pre-flop or retain initiative post-flop. I will raise to 300.

I hold 2s 8s in the Big Blind. This is a very weak hand (8-high, poor connectivity), although suited. The opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3x the big blind) from the Button. While I am getting decent pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity), the playability of 82s out of position is very poor. It will be difficult to realize that equity. Against a 3x open, 82s is a standard fold in heads-up play.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with J2o. In Heads-Up play, the Button should open a wide range, but J2o is near the very bottom of the starting hand chart due to poor connectivity and a weak kicker. Looking at my history, I have already folded similar weak hands (T2o, 94o, 62o) in this position, playing a tighter strategy (approx. 40% VPIP on button so far). While I need to open up my range eventually to avoid being exploited, J2o is not a profitable hand to do so with, even in position. Folding saves chips for better spots.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 5d 3c, which is a very weak hand. While heads-up strategy often involves playing a wide range of hands, 53o is near the bottom of that range. Given that my opponent 'Pro' has shown a mix of folding and defending, I don't see much value in completing the blind or attempting a steal with such poor equity. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with Js9c. In heads-up play, this is a playable hand (roughly top 45% of hands). Looking at my previous history, I have folded my button quite often (Hands 1, 3, 7, 11), which is too tight. I need to open wider to avoid being exploited. My opponent has folded to my previous raises (Hands 5, 9). A min-raise to 200 allows me to potentially take down the blinds immediately or play a pot in position with a hand that has decent connectivity and high card strength.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 4s, a very weak hand. The Button (Pro) has raised to 200. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call (100 into a 300 pot), 84 offsuit has very poor playability and realization of equity. In a heads-up match, I should defend a wide range, but this specific hand is at the bottom of the range and likely to lead to difficult post-flop spots. I will fold and wait for a better hand to contest.

Holding K4 suited on the button in a heads-up match is a strong opening candidate. I want to take control of the hand and apply pressure to the Big Blind. A standard raise to 3x the big blind (300 total) is a balanced play here that allows me to win the pot immediately or play a pot with position and a hand that has decent post-flop playability.

I hold Tc 4s in the Big Blind. My opponent (Button) has raised to 300 (3BB). This is an unsuited, disconnected hand with poor playability out of position. While T4o might technically have near break-even raw equity against a very wide opening range, the realizeable equity is significantly lower due to being out of position against a 3x size. Defending here is likely -EV compared to folding. I will fold.

In heads-up play, holding an Ace on the button is statistically strong and warrants an open raise. My hand (A4o) beats a random hand comfortably. The opponent has shown a tendency to fold to my button raises in previous hands (Hands 5, 9, 13), making a steal attempt profitable. A minimum raise to 200 is standard to apply pressure while minimizing risk.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 7s, a suited gapper that plays reasonably well post-flop. The Button has raised to 200, requiring me to call 100 more into a pot of 300. This gives me 3:1 pot odds, which is more than enough to justify a defend with this hand. While I could 3-bet as a bluff, calling allows me to realize my equity and play a pot in-position or out-of-position depending on the flop texture, without bloating the pot unnecessarily against a potentially strong range.

On this Ace-high board (A-2-3), the pre-flop raiser (Pro) has a significant range advantage as they are much more likely to hold an Ace than I am. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Checking is the standard play here to see how the aggressor acts. Donk betting into an Ace-high board is usually ill-advised against a wide button-opening range.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (4) and a gutshot straight draw on a [2s Ah 3d] board. While I have a strong hand for heads-up play, my kicker leaves me vulnerable to better Aces. The board texture interacts well with the Big Blind's calling range (low pairs, straight draws). Betting risks isolating myself against better hands or facing a check-raise, which would put me in a difficult spot. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity (including the straight draw), and potentially induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. It also strengthens my checking range. I plan to call reasonable bets on the turn or bet for value if checked to again.

On the turn, the board has paired the deuce. After the flop went check-check, the pre-flop raiser's range is somewhat capped, but they still hold the range advantage on an Ace-high board. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Leading here (donk betting) doesn't achieve much since any Ace or pocket pair will likely call, and I have no showdown value. I will check to see how the opponent responds; if they check back, I get a free card to try and hit my straight. If they bet, I can evaluate my pot odds to call with my gutshot.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker and a gutshot wheel draw on a paired board [2s Ah 3d 2c]. After checking back the flop, checking the turn again is the prudent play for several reasons. First, it exercises pot control; with a weak kicker, I am vulnerable to better Aces or trips (2x), and I want to avoid facing a check-raise which would force me to fold my equity. Second, checking back under-represents my hand strength, potentially inducing bluffs from the opponent on the river or allowing me to get thin value from worse pairs that might call a river bet but fold to a turn bet. Third, I have a draw to a straight (5) or two pair (4) that I can realize for free. My plan is to check back, then call a reasonable bet on the river or value bet if checked to again.

The board has run out A-2-3-2-J. I have 7-high with a missed gutshot. The action has been checked down since the pre-flop call. The pre-flop raiser (Pro) checked both the flop and the turn, suggesting they likely don't have a strong Ace or a monster. However, even with a capped range, they might have a small pocket pair, a Jack that hit on the river, or simply a better high card than my 7-high. Since there is no bet to call, I will check. Attempting a bluff here is risky because the pot is small and my opponent's range has enough 'showdown value' (like K-high or small pairs) that might call a small bet, and I'd rather take the free showdown than risk chips with zero value.

I hold Top Pair (Aces) with a weak kicker (4). The action has been checked through on the flop and turn, and the opponent checked the river. While my hand is likely the winner against the opponent's range of lower pairs (Jacks, 3s, pocket pairs), betting is thin. Any better Ace (A5+) beats me, and hands worse than mine (like King-high or small pairs) might not call a bet. Betting risks value-owning myself against a better Ace that also played passively or opening myself to a check-raise from a trapped monster (like a 2). Checking back is the prudent play to secure the pot against worse hands without additional risk.
I am on the button with Ace-Six of hearts, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take control of the hand.

Opponent opens to 3bb (300) from the Button. I hold K5o in the Big Blind. While K5o has reasonable raw equity against a wide Heads-Up opening range, the larger 3x raise size worsens my pot odds (requiring 33% equity) and makes realizing equity out of position difficult. Poor playability and reverse implied odds with a weak kicker suggest folding is the prudent play against this sizing.

I hold K8s in the Small Blind. In heads-up play, this is a strong starting hand, well within the top range of hands to play. My opponent, Flash, has folded to my pre-flop raises in 3 out of the 4 times I have raised from the SB (Hands 5, 9, 13), calling only once (Hand 15). This suggests they are currently over-folding their Big Blind. A min-raise to 200 is the most effective play here to steal the blind or play a pot in position with a hand that has good playability (flush and straight potential, high card strength).

I am holding Ten-Nine suited, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play with excellent post-flop playability. The opponent has raised to 2x (200) from the button. I am getting 3:1 odds to call. Given the strength of my hand and the position, I will call to see a flop and utilize the drawing potential of these suited connectors.

I have hit top pair on a relatively dry but somewhat connected board. In heads-up play, top pair with a nine kicker is a very strong hand. As I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to them and see how they respond. This allows them to bluff with missed overcards or continue with their range while keeping the pot manageable or setting up a check-call.

I hold Middle Pair (Eights) with a King kicker on a board of [2h, Td, 8d]. The board is somewhat wet with a flush draw and straight possibilities. My opponent checked to me. While I have showdown value, betting opens me up to check-raises which would put me in a difficult spot. Betting also isolates me against better hands like Top Pair (Tx). By checking back, I control the pot size, disguise the strength of my hand, and keep my opponent's range wide, allowing me to potentially call a bet on the turn or bet for value later if checked to again. This line minimizes risk while retaining the ability to win at showdown.

I have top pair with a nine kicker. The flop went check-check after Pro raised pre-flop, which suggests Pro might not have a very strong hand or is playing a medium-strength hand cautiously. The turn card is the Four of Diamonds, completing the flush draw from the flop. While I have a solid hand, the presence of three diamonds makes the board more dangerous. I will check again to see if Pro bets. If Pro checks back again, it's likely my pair of Tens is the best hand; if Pro bets, I will have to consider the sizing and the likelihood they have a diamond or a slow-played monster.

I hold Middle Pair (Eights) with a strong King kicker on a board of [2h, Td, 8d, 4d]. The arrival of the 4d puts three diamonds on the board, making a flush a real possibility. Since I do not hold a diamond, my hand is vulnerable. Betting here is thin for value because worse hands will likely fold while better hands (Tx, flush, two pair) will call or check-raise. By checking back, I exercise pot control, avoid getting blown off my equity by a check-raise, and keep my opponent's range wide. This line allows me to see the river for free and re-evaluate, aiming to get to showdown cheaply or catch a bluff on the river.

I have improved to top two pair on the river with Tens and Nines. The action so far (check-check on both the flop and the turn) suggests that my opponent likely does not have a made flush or a very strong hand. By betting now, I can extract value from a single pair like a Ten, an Eight, or even a smaller pocket pair that might call a moderate bet. A half-pot bet is a standard sizing here to maximize value without folding out too many of the opponent's weaker hands.

I hold K8s on a board of [2h, Td, 8d, 4d, 9c]. I have a pair of Eights, which is now the third pair on the board after the river Nine. My opponent, Flash, called pre-flop and checked the flop and turn. Their bet of 200 (half-pot) on the river, after being passive previously, suggests thin value—likely a Ten or a rivered pair of Nines—rather than a bluff. A bluff would typically be larger to generate fold equity. Since the river card is an overcard to my pair and improves many hands in a calling range (like J9, Q9, A9), my hand is unlikely to be good often enough to justify calling, even with 3:1 pot odds. Preserving my stack is the better long-term play here.

I am on the button with Ace-Deuce suited, a premium hand in heads-up play. My opponent, Pro, has shown a tendency to fold to my pre-flop raises from the button (Hands 6, 10, 14, 16). I will continue with my established sizing of 3x the big blind (300 chips) to build the pot and take advantage of my positional and hand strength.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit (Kh Tc), facing a 3x raise to 300 from the Button (Flash). In Heads-Up play, KTo is a strong hand well above the median. Flash has been raising to 300 frequently (approx 66% of buttons shown), indicating a reasonably wide range. I need to call 200 chips to win a total pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. KTo has approximately 60% equity against a random hand and plays well enough against a button opening range. Folding is incorrect due to hand strength, and while 3-betting is an option, calling is the standard low-variance play to keep the opponent's wide range involved while playing out of position. I will call the additional 200.

I hold KTo on a 7h 5d Ac flop. The board is very dry but contains an Ace, which favors the pre-flop aggressor's (Flash) range significantly. Being out of position with King-high and no draw, I have no incentive to lead (donk bet). Leading would likely only get called by better hands (Ax, pairs) and fold out worse hands that I might beat at showdown or that might bluff later. The standard play is to check to the raiser. If the opponent bets, I will evaluate, but checking is the clear first step to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or catch a card.

I have top pair with an Ace on a relatively dry board (7h-5d-Ac). Pro checked to me, suggesting they may have missed or are holding a marginal hand. I will bet 300 (half pot) to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs or gutshot draws, and to protect my hand.

I hold King-Ten offsuit on a dry Ace-Seven-Five board (Ac 7h 5d). I checked to the pre-flop raiser, who has now made a continuation bet of 300 (half pot). This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as they hold all the strong Aces, while my defending range is capped. With only King-high, no flush draw, and no straight draw, my hand has very little equity against any pair or better Ace. Floating out of position with King-high is a losing strategy without a strong specific read on the opponent's frequencies. The prudent play is to fold and preserve chips.

I hold King-Ace offsuit (Ks Ac) on the Button, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. My strategy is to build the pot for value while maintaining my standard raising frequency to disguise hand strength. In previous hands (#5, #9, #13, #15, #17), I have consistently opened to 200. Deviating now might alert the opponent. Therefore, I will raise to 200. This extracts value from worse hands and allows me to play a large pot in position post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace of Hearts, Eight of Hearts, which is a very strong starting hand in heads-up play. Pro has raised from the Button/Small Blind to 200. I will 3-bet to 600 total to build the pot and take the initiative, as my hand is well ahead of a typical button raising range.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand, on the Button. After opening to 200, the opponent has 3-bet to 600. This is the first 3-bet I have faced this match. While a 4-bet is a viable option to build the pot immediately, calling in position is often superior in Heads-Up play with deep stacks (90bb). Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, including bluffs and dominated hands like AQ/AJ/KQ, which might fold to a 4-bet. It also allows me to use my position to navigate post-flop without inflating the pot excessively before seeing community cards. I will call the additional 400 to see a flop.

I have the nut flush draw and an overcard on a board of Tc 7h 3h. I was the pre-flop 3-better, and I should maintain the initiative by firing a continuation bet. This bet can fold out hands like small pairs or missed high cards, and even if called, I have significant equity to improve on the turn or river.

I hold Ace-King offsuit on a T-7-3 flop with two hearts. My opponent has c-bet 600 (half pot) into a 3-bet pot. This is the first time the opponent has 3-bet in the match, suggesting a strong range likely consisting of premium pairs (TT+, JJ+) or strong broadways (AK, AQs). My hand has missed the board completely, and I do not hold a flush draw (my Ace is clubs, King is spades). Against a range of overpairs, I have very little equity (~12-15%) and will face difficult decisions on later streets even if I am currently ahead of a hand like AQ. Floating with just overcards in a 3-bet pot without a strong backdoor draw is generally -EV, especially against an opponent showing strength. I will fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 7h 5d. Although it is a weak hand, Pro has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop steals and early street aggression in recent hands. I will continue with my strategy of applying pressure from the button by raising to 300.

The opponent raises to 300 (3BB) from the button, continuing a pattern of frequent 3x opens. While my pot odds are 2:1 (calling 200 to win 600), requiring 33% equity, Q3o is a very weak hand with poor playability out of position. Even against a wide range, realizing equity will be difficult against an aggressive opponent who frequently c-bets. Defending Q3o against a 3x raise is generally -EV. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the Button (SB) with K9o, which is an above-average hand in heads-up play (top ~28-30% of hands). My stack is deep (85 BB). I have position post-flop. My standard opening size has been a min-raise to 200, which has generated folds or allowed me to play pots in position. Folding is too tight, and limping invites aggression from the Big Blind without defining ranges. Raising to 200 extracts value from worse hands and denies equity to random junk.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Pro has opened for a min-raise (200) from the Button/Small Blind. A7o is a very strong hand in a heads-up situation, well ahead of a typical wide opening range from the Small Blind. To maximize value and take control of the hand out of position, I will 3-bet to 600.

I am holding King-Nine offsuit on the Button and facing a 3-bet from the Big Blind (Flash) to 600 chips. Although K9o is a reasonable hand to open in heads-up play, it performs poorly against a 3-betting range, often being dominated by better Kings (AK, KQ, KJ, KT) or Aces. I need 33% equity to call, but my equity realization will be difficult even in position against an aggressive opponent who has shown a tendency to bet strongly post-flop (as seen in Hand #19). With stacks deep (83 BBs), avoiding reverse implied odds scenarios with marginal hands is prudent. Folding here is the standard play to preserve chips for better spots.

I am on the button with Q9s, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to apply pressure and build a pot in position. I have consistently used a 3x open to 300 in previous hands, and I will maintain that sizing here to stay balanced and exploit my positional advantage.

I hold 5c 4h in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. My opponent has been consistently opening to 300. While 54o has some connectivity, it is an off-suit low connector and performs poorly out of position, especially against a larger-than-minimum raise size. The pot odds require me to call 200 to win a pot of 600 (33%), but realizing that equity OOP with this specific hand is difficult. Given the stack depth and the weak nature of my holding, the most profitable long-term play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I hold Q7o on the Button (SB), which is a hand with approximately 51.5% equity against a random hand. In Heads-Up play, this is a standard opening hand. Reviewing recent history, the opponent (Flash) has started 3-betting my opens in the last few orbits (Hands 19 and 21), but prior to that, they folded to my raises frequently (Hands 5, 9, 13). While the recent aggression is noted, abandoning a standard button open with a playable hand like Q7o is too passive. I will min-raise to 200 to apply pressure and utilize my position. If faced with a 3-bet, this hand is an easy fold.

I am in the big blind with 9s2c. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the small blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, but 92o is extremely weak and plays poorly post-flop, especially out of position. I have a healthy chip lead (11,650 vs 8,050). In heads-up, defense ranges are wider, but 92o is typically a fold even against a min-raise. I will choose to fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with King-Six of hearts, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. I have a significant chip lead (11,600 vs 8,250). In previous hands, I have been playing aggressively from the button, often raising to 300. My opponent, Pro, has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop aggression or small continuation bets. Raising here allows me to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot immediately or set up a post-flop play with a hand that has good flush and top-pair potential.

I am in the Big Blind with K9s, a strong suited hand in heads-up play. My opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 300 (3bb), which they have done frequently. I need to call 200 more to win a pot that will be 600 total, giving me 2:1 pot odds. I need roughly 33% equity to call profitably. K9s has significantly higher equity (around 50-55%) against a standard wide button opening range. Although I will be out of position post-flop, folding is too tight given the hand strength, and calling is a standard defense to realize my equity.

I have flopped Top Pair (Kings) with a decent kicker (9) on a K-4-3 board with a flush draw present. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Flash). This allows the opponent to continue with their c-betting frequency, which has been high in previous hands, effectively inducing bluffs or value bets from worse hands. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their air and only get action from strong hands. My plan is to check and comfortably call any standard bet to keep their range wide.

I have flopped top pair with a King on a dry board of K-4-3. Pro checked to me, and since I am the pre-flop aggressor and have the best hand most of the time here, I should make a continuation bet. A bet of around half the pot (300) is standard to get value from weaker hands like 4x, 3x, or middle pocket pairs, while also protecting against any spade draws or overcards. Pro has been folding to post-flop aggression in previous hands, so this is a good spot to build the pot or take it down.

I hold Top Pair with a Nine kicker on a K-4-3 board with two spades. My opponent has c-bet 300 (half-pot) after raising pre-flop. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong hand. Folding is incorrect as I am well ahead of their bluffing range and many worse value hands (lower pairs, draws). Raising might fold out their bluffs, which I want to keep in the pot, or isolate me against better hands like AK/KQ or sets. Calling is the best option to control the pot size out of position and allow the opponent to continue bluffing or value betting worse hands on future streets.

I hold Top Pair with a King and a 9 kicker on a board of K-4-3-3. The turn card pairs the board with the 3h, which is generally a brick for the opponent's wide button range unless they hold a 3. As the pre-flop caller out of position, my standard play is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) would likely fold out their bluffs (air, missed draws) and only get action from hands that beat me or have strong equity. By checking, I allow the opponent to continue bluffing or value betting a wider range. If they bet, I intend to call given my hand strength in a heads-up scenario.

I have top pair (Kings) with a six kicker. The turn card is a Trey, pairing the board. Pro has checked to me again after calling my flop continuation bet. Since Pro called the flop, he likely has a piece of the board (like a 4 or a weaker King) or a draw (like spades or a gutshot). I will bet 600, which is half the pot, to extract value from those weaker hands and to charge any draws. If Pro raises, I will have to consider the possibility that he has a better King or has trip Threes, but for now, betting for value is the most logical step.

I hold Top Pair (Kings) with a 9 kicker on a board of K-4-3-3.
